heroOverview: Runner's knee — clinically known as patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) — is the most common running injury and the single most frequent knee complaint in sports medicine clinics, accounting for 25-40% of all knee problems presenting to practitioners. It produces aching or burning pain around or behind the kneecap (patella) that worsens with prolonged sitting, squatting, stairs, downhill running, and jumping. The pain arises from increased stress and abnormal load distribution across the patellofemoral joint — the joint between the patella and the groove at the front of the femur — and is driven by a combination of biomechanical, muscular, and training-load factors rather than structural damage. With targeted rehabilitation centred on hip and quadriceps strengthening, load management, and biomechanical correction, the majority of runners achieve significant symptom reduction within 6-12 weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: Accounts for 25-40% of all knee injuries seen in sports medicine; prevalence in recreational runners approximately 7-15% per year (BJSM, 2019)
keyStats.demographics: Twice as common in women as in men; most prevalent in runners aged 15-35, but affects all ages and activity levels from recreational walkers to competitive athletes
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset in adolescents and young adults aged 15-35; increasingly recognised in older recreational exercisers
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 30-40 million episodes annually in the United States alone, making it one of the most prevalent overuse musculoskeletal complaints

definition: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS; ICD-10 approximate: M22.4, chondromalacia patellae; M79.36, pain in knee) is a clinical diagnosis characterised by peripatellar or retropatellar pain that is reproducibly aggravated by activities loading the patellofemoral joint under compression. The patellofemoral joint transmits forces two to four times body weight during level running and up to seven times body weight during squatting or stair descent, concentrating stress on the retropatellar cartilage and the subchondral bone. Pain is believed to arise from a combination of elevated intra-articular pressure, subchondral bone stress, retinacular mechanoreceptor irritation, and synovial inflammation — rather than overt cartilage damage, which distinguishes PFPS from true chondromalacia (cartilage softening). The biomechanical factors that increase patellofemoral joint stress include weak or delayed activation of the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) component of the quadriceps, weak hip abductors and external rotators, excessive femoral internal rotation and adduction during loading, rearfoot eversion, and tight iliotibial band or lateral retinaculum. Training errors — rapid increases in mileage, introduction of hill work or interval sessions, and sudden changes in footwear or running surface — are the most common precipitating events. PFPS is managed principally by sports medicine physicians and physiotherapists; structural pathology requiring surgical opinion is uncommon.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation: diffuse peripatellar pain without structural cartilage damage, driven by biomechanical loading abnormalities and training errors. Responds well to rehabilitation.

Softening, fibrillation, and breakdown of the articular cartilage on the underside of the patella, confirmed on MRI or arthroscopy. Overlaps clinically with PFPS but represents true structural damage; most cases still respond to conservative treatment.
A subset in which the patella is excessively tilted and compressed laterally by a tight lateral retinaculum, producing lateral retropatellar pain and lateral patellar tilt on axial radiography.
Recurrent patellar subluxation or dislocation episodes produce retropatellar pain, apprehension, and medial peripatellar tenderness from medial patellofemoral ligament stretch or tear.

primarySymptoms: A dull, aching, or burning pain around the front of the knee, typically felt behind or around the edges of the kneecap rather than in a specific focal point.
Pain during or after sustained sitting with the knee bent (the 'theatre sign' or 'cinema sign'), caused by elevated patellofemoral joint pressure held statically for a prolonged period.
Worsening pain on descending stairs or running downhill, where the quadriceps must eccentrically control knee flexion under high patellofemoral load.
Pain during squatting, lunging, or kneeling — activities that bend the knee under body weight and compress the patella against the femoral trochlea.
Pain during or after running, particularly on prolonged runs, interval sessions, or after sudden increases in training volume.
A sensation of crepitus or grinding under the kneecap when the knee is flexed and extended, felt with a hand over the patella.
Peripatellar stiffness and achiness after rest following activity — the 'start-up' stiffness that eases within a few minutes of moving.
Tenderness when pressing on the medial or lateral facets of the patella with the knee relaxed.
Swelling around the kneecap — mild diffuse periarticular swelling can be present in more acute presentations, though significant effusion suggests alternative pathology.
Quadriceps weakness or a feeling of the knee 'giving way' on stairs or uneven ground, reflecting inhibitory muscle dysfunction from pain.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild aching behind the kneecap noticed during the last few kilometres of long runs before frank pain appears.
Discomfort when sitting still for 30-60 minutes in a cinema, car, or at a desk that prompts the runner to frequently extend the leg.
A gradual increase in perceived effort or discomfort on stairways that was not present a few weeks earlier.
Subtle quadriceps fatigue or a feeling of the knee being 'off' after runs, without obvious pain, suggesting early load tolerance reduction.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe pain with a 'pop' during a jump or direction change, suggesting patellar dislocation or ligament rupture — requires same-day assessment.
Significant knee effusion (visible swelling of the joint line), which is not typical of PFPS and raises concern for meniscal injury, intra-articular ligament tear, or osteochondral lesion.
Inability to bear weight on the knee after a traumatic episode.
Persistent locking or true mechanical blocking of knee extension, which suggests a loose body or meniscal tear rather than PFPS.

causes.cause: Abnormal patellar tracking from muscle imbalance
causes.description: Weakness or delayed activation of the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) allows the patella to track laterally in the femoral trochlea during dynamic loading, concentrating contact pressure on the lateral facet. Concurrent weakness of the hip abductors and external rotators allows femoral internal rotation, further lateralising patellar contact stress.

Sudden increases in weekly running mileage, introduction of interval or hill sessions, or adding a second sport exceed the patellofemoral joint's capacity to adapt. The rate of load increase — not total load alone — is the key driver, consistent with acute-to-chronic workload ratio principles in sports medicine.

causes.cause: Hip weakness and abnormal lower limb kinematics
causes.description: Weakness of the hip abductors and external rotators (gluteus medius and minimus) allows contralateral pelvic drop and ipsilateral femoral adduction and internal rotation during the stance phase of running, increasing the Q-angle and dynamic valgus loading at the patellofemoral joint.

causes.cause: Tight lateral retinaculum or iliotibial band
causes.description: An excessively tight lateral retinaculum pulls the patella laterally and increases compressive force on the lateral patellar facet. Iliotibial band tightness similarly lateralises patellar forces via its connection to the lateral retinaculum.

Excessive subtalar pronation at the foot increases tibial internal rotation during stance, rotating the femoral trochlea away from the patella and amplifying patellofemoral contact stress. Orthotic intervention targeting foot mechanics can reduce pain in a subset of patients.

Trochlear dysplasia (a shallow or asymmetric femoral groove), patella alta (a high-riding patella), and excessive Q-angle from increased hip offset or tibial torsion are structural factors that reduce inherent patellofemoral stability and predispose to pain with loading.

Reduced quadriceps strength — relative to body weight and to contralateral limb — reduces the muscle's shock-absorbing capacity and shifts more load directly onto the patellofemoral cartilage and subchondral bone during impact activities.

riskFactors.description: Women have approximately twice the incidence of PFPS as men; contributing anatomical factors include a wider pelvis increasing the Q-angle, ligamentous laxity, and differences in quadriceps and hip muscle strength patterns.

Increasing running volume by more than 10% per week, adding back-to-back long runs, or introducing speed work without adequate base conditioning consistently identifies as the most common modifiable precipitant in epidemiological studies of running injuries.

riskFactors.factor: Hip abductor and external rotator weakness
riskFactors.description: Objectively measurable weakness in hip abductors and external rotators (gluteus medius, piriformis) is found in 60-70% of runners with PFPS and precedes rather than follows the injury onset in prospective studies, making it both a risk factor and a treatment target.

A high-riding patella (assessed by the Caton-Deschamps index on lateral radiograph) and shallow trochlear groove reduce bony constraint of patellar tracking and raise peak contact pressure at the same knee flexion angle.
A history of patellar dislocation, ACL injury, or prior knee surgery alters patellofemoral joint biomechanics and muscle firing patterns, raising PFPS risk by approximately two- to threefold.

riskFactors.factor: Running on hard surfaces or with worn footwear
riskFactors.description: Running predominantly on asphalt or concrete without adequate midsole cushioning increases peak vertical loading rate at the patellofemoral joint; replacing shoes every 500-700 km maintains appropriate shock attenuation.

Rapid bone growth during puberty temporarily outpaces muscle and tendon lengthening, increasing tightness of the quadriceps and iliotibial band relative to a rapidly lengthening femur, predisposing adolescent athletes to PFPS.

riskFactors.factor: Excessive forward trunk lean or foot strike pattern
riskFactors.description: An anterior trunk lean during running increases the knee flexion angle at initial contact, amplifying patellofemoral joint reaction force; a forefoot or midfoot strike reduces peak patellofemoral loading in some studies compared with heavy heel striking.

affectedDemographics: Recreational and competitive runners of all distances, particularly those training for half-marathons and marathons
Adolescent athletes in jumping and multidirectional sports (basketball, volleyball, football)
Women aged 15-35, who have approximately twice the incidence of men
Military recruits undergoing high-volume rapid load increases during basic training
Cyclists who position the saddle too low, increasing knee flexion angle and patellofemoral compression
Older recreational exercisers returning to running after a long hiatus
diagnosisOverview: Runner's knee is a clinical diagnosis made from the history and examination, without requiring imaging in most cases. The diagnostic hallmarks are anterior knee pain that is consistently reproduced by activities loading the patellofemoral joint — prolonged sitting, squatting, stairs, and running — and peripatellar or retropatellar tenderness on palpation of the patellar facets with the knee extended and relaxed. The Clarke test (resisted patellar compression with active quadriceps contraction) may reproduce pain but has poor specificity. More reliable examination findings include pain with single-leg squat, tenderness on patellar facet palpation, and a positive patellar glide test showing lateral hypomobility or lateral tilt. Leg and hip biomechanics are assessed: contralateral pelvic drop, knee-over-toe alignment during a single-leg squat, and foot pronation all point to hip and lower limb kinematic contributors. In the absence of trauma, effusion, locking, or instability, plain radiographs are not routinely required at first presentation. Lateral X-ray is indicated if patella alta or trochlear dysplasia is clinically suspected, and axial (Merchant) views assess lateral patellar tilt. MRI is reserved for cases not responding to 8-12 weeks of structured rehabilitation, to exclude meniscal pathology, osteochondral lesions, or patellar stress fracture. Differential diagnoses to consider include iliotibial band syndrome (lateral knee pain peaking at 30 degrees flexion), patellar tendinopathy (focal inferior pole pain with jumping), Osgood-Schlatter disease (tibial tubercle pain in adolescents), plica syndrome (medial parapatellar band snapping and tenderness), and pes anserine bursitis.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination and single-leg squat assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduces symptoms under load and assesses the biomechanical contributors — hip drop, knee valgus, foot pronation — that drive patellofemoral malalignment, guiding rehabilitation targets.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician observes you performing a single-leg squat from the front and side; a slow squat to 60 degrees of knee flexion reliably reproduces PFPS symptoms and reveals kinematic faults. No equipment needed; takes about 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Patellar facet palpation and provocation tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies tenderness on the medial and lateral patellar facets, confirming patellofemoral involvement rather than patellar tendon or meniscal pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician presses on the edges of the kneecap with your knee relaxed; pressure on one or both facets reproduces the familiar pain. Mild discomfort is expected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses patella alta (Caton-Deschamps index) and trochlear dysplasia, and excludes bony pathology such as patellar stress fracture or osteoarthritis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand with the knee slightly flexed; the X-ray takes seconds and involves minimal radiation. Results are usually available the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates lateral patellar tilt and the depth of the trochlear groove; useful when lateral compression syndrome or instability is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie on the X-ray table with knees bent to 45 degrees; the X-ray is taken looking along the knee from the foot end; takes about five minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes PFPS from structural pathology — meniscal tears, osteochondral lesions, bone stress injuries, or early osteoarthritis — in runners not responding to 8-12 weeks of rehabilitation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a scanner for 30-45 minutes; no contrast injection is usually needed for soft tissue assessment of the patellofemoral joint. Results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Hip and lower limb strength testing (dynamometry or clinical)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies hip abductor, external rotator, and quadriceps strength deficits that drive patellofemoral biomechanics, and identifies the side-to-side asymmetry that guides rehabilitation priorities.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A physiotherapist or sports medicine physician manually or dynamometrically tests specific muscle groups; the session takes 15-20 minutes and forms the basis of the rehabilitation prescription.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of runner's knee treatment is a structured progressive rehabilitation programme rather than passive rest or imaging-led intervention. Current evidence from systematic reviews and the 2019 British Journal of Sports Medicine consensus supports hip- and knee-focused strength training, activity modification, and biomechanical correction as the primary treatments, with short-term pain relief adjuncts used in the early phase. In the acute phase (weeks 1-3), running volume is reduced — typically by 50% or to a level that keeps pain below 3/10 on a numeric scale — rather than stopped entirely. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen provide short-term symptomatic relief in the acute period and can allow earlier engagement with rehabilitation but do not address the underlying cause. Patellar taping (McConnell technique) and patellar bracing with a lateral buttress redirect patellofemoral load distribution and provide immediate pain reduction of approximately 30-50% in many patients, enabling them to exercise at higher loads during rehabilitation. The rehabilitation programme targets: quadriceps strengthening (emphasising VMO activation through short-arc and terminal extension exercises), hip abductor and external rotator strengthening (side-lying hip abduction, clamshells, single-leg bridges, resistance band work), and progressive functional exercises (step-downs, single-leg squats, lunges). Foot orthotics — pre-fabricated or custom — are beneficial in the subset of patients with significant rearfoot eversion, reducing peak patellofemoral joint stress. Running form retraining, including cadence increase (typically to 170-180 steps per minute), reduction of overstriding, and cues to reduce contralateral pelvic drop, reduces patellofemoral load by 14-25% in biomechanical studies. Return to full running is a graduated process over 6-12 weeks. Corticosteroid injection into the patellofemoral joint has limited and short-lived evidence of benefit and is not a primary recommendation. The overall prognosis is favourable: approximately 80-90% of patients achieve substantial long-term improvement with conservative management.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs reduce pain and periarticular inflammation in the acute phase, allowing the runner to begin rehabilitation. They are recommended for the shortest effective duration — typically 1-2 weeks — because they do not address the mechanical cause and long-term use carries gastrointestinal and renal risks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide 30-50% pain reduction in acute PFPS versus placebo; clinical trials show equivalent outcomes to other analgesics at 4-6 weeks but no long-term benefit over rehabilitation alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Patellar taping (McConnell technique)
medicalTreatments.description: Rigid sports tape applied to medially glide and tilt the patella corrects its tracking angle and redistributes articular contact, allowing training at greater knee flexion with lower pain scores. Commonly used in the first 4-8 weeks of rehabilitation to break the pain-inhibition cycle.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain by approximately 30-50% immediately post-application in randomised trials; clinically significant improvement in exercise capacity during rehabilitation reported in most studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Patellar bracing with lateral buttress
medicalTreatments.description: An off-the-shelf or custom knee sleeve with a lateral patellar pad or buttress that limits lateral patellar displacement during activity; provides consistent proprioceptive input and pain relief, particularly on stairs and downhill running.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 25-40% immediate pain reduction; non-inferior to patellar taping in direct comparisons with better patient adherence for daily running activity.

medicalTreatments.name: Hip and quadriceps rehabilitation programme
medicalTreatments.description: A structured 8-12 week programme targeting hip abductor, external rotator, and quadriceps strength and activation is the highest-evidence intervention for PFPS. Hip-focused exercises (clamshells, lateral band walks, single-leg bridges) combined with terminal knee extension and step-down training produce the most durable improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Systematic reviews report 60-80% of patients achieve clinically significant improvement in pain and function at 3-6 months; hip-targeted programmes produce superior results to knee-only protocols.

medicalTreatments.name: Foot orthotics
medicalTreatments.description: Pre-fabricated or custom-made insoles that support the medial arch and control rearfoot eversion reduce tibial internal rotation and subsequent patellofemoral joint stress. Most beneficial in runners with measurable foot pronation contributing to their biomechanical presentation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce patellofemoral pain scores by an average of 25-35% in runners with rearfoot valgus; Cochrane review supports short-term benefit; combined with exercise produces better outcomes than orthosis alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Running form retraining (gait retraining)
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing step rate (cadence) to 170-180 steps per minute, shortening stride length to reduce overstriding, and cueing 'softer' landing reduces peak patellofemoral joint reaction force by 14-25% in controlled biomechanical studies. Real-time feedback using music or a metronome at the target cadence accelerates the relearning process.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dry needling and acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Trigger point dry needling of the quadriceps, hip abductors, and iliotibial band has small randomised trial evidence for short-term pain reduction as an adjunct to exercise therapy; acupuncture shows similar modest short-term benefit. Neither replaces exercise rehabilitation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Blood-flow restriction (BFR) training
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-load quadriceps and hip exercises performed with a blood-flow restriction cuff produce hypertrophy and strength gains equivalent to high-load training but at significantly lower joint loading; particularly useful in the acute painful phase when high-load exercise exacerbates symptoms.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cases of runner's knee can be managed by a physiotherapist experienced in running injuries without a specialist physician. Referral to a sports medicine physician or orthopaedic surgeon is indicated when pain persists despite 12 weeks of structured rehabilitation, when imaging identifies structural pathology such as an osteochondral lesion or patellar fracture, when patellar instability or recurrent dislocation is present, or when an anatomical abnormality such as severe trochlear dysplasia or significant patella alta is suspected to be driving the condition.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a physiotherapist with a post-graduate qualification in sports physiotherapy and experience assessing running biomechanics; ideally one who offers video gait analysis. A sports medicine physician (with FSEM, SEM, or equivalent qualification) is valuable for diagnostic uncertainty, injection decisions, or return-to-sport clearance. Ask whether the physiotherapist performs a hip strength assessment and biomechanical analysis, as these are the evidence-based drivers of treatment decisions. Clinicians affiliated with running clubs or academic sports medicine centres typically have the most current evidence base.
preventionStrategies: Follow the 10% rule — increase weekly running mileage by no more than 10% per week to avoid sudden load spikes at the patellofemoral joint.

Strengthen the hip abductors and external rotators as a prehabilitation component of any running programme; studies show even 6 weeks of hip strengthening significantly reduces PFPS incidence in high-risk groups.
Replace running shoes every 500-700 km; worn midsoles lose shock attenuation and increase peak vertical loading rates.
Vary running surface where possible; mix softer trails with road running to reduce cumulative patellofemoral load.
Warm up adequately before runs and cool down and stretch the quadriceps, hip flexors, and iliotibial band routinely.
Monitor running cadence and aim for 170-180 steps per minute to reduce overstriding and knee flexion angle at initial contact.
Address rearfoot eversion with pre-fabricated orthotics early if foot mechanics are a contributing factor.
lifestyleModifications: Reduce but do not entirely stop running during flare-ups; maintain fitness with low-impact cross-training such as swimming, pool running, or cycling with the saddle adjusted high.
Avoid prolonged sitting with knees bent for more than 30-40 minutes at a time; take brief walking breaks to relieve sustained patellofemoral compression.
Apply ice (wrapped in a cloth) to the knee for 15-20 minutes after exercise if local pain and swelling are present.
Maintain a healthy body weight; a 5 kg increase in body weight increases patellofemoral joint reaction force by approximately 20-35 N during level walking.
Prioritise 7-9 hours of sleep to support tissue repair and adaptation between training sessions.
Monitor training load with a running app and track the acute-to-chronic workload ratio to identify dangerous loading spikes before symptoms develop.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.6-2.0 g/kg body weight per day) to support muscle hypertrophy during rehabilitation strength training
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish, walnuts, or flaxseed to support reduction of periarticular inflammation
Calcium and vitamin D from dairy, fortified foods, or supplements to support bone health and subchondral bone adaptation
Anti-inflammatory foods: colourful vegetables, berries, and olive oil as components of a balanced training diet
avoid: Excess body weight through calorie surplus; each kilogram of excess mass adds 4-5 kg of patellofemoral joint reaction force per step
Heavy alcohol consumption, which impairs muscle protein synthesis, sleep quality, and tissue repair
NSAIDs on an empty stomach; take with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects during short-term use in the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: During active PFPS, reduce running volume to the level at which pain stays below 3/10 on a numerical rating scale; this is usually 40-60% of previous volume. Substitute with pool running, cycling (with raised saddle to reduce knee flexion), swimming, or elliptical trainer to maintain aerobic fitness. Resistance training should be progressed through: isometric exercises (wall sit at 60 degrees, isometric VMO holds) in the acute phase; isotonic exercises (terminal knee extension, short-arc quads, step-downs) once pain is below 3/10; and plyometric and sport-specific training only when pain-free and within 10% of contralateral limb strength. Eccentric quad and hip exercises (eccentric step-downs, Nordic curls) produce superior strength gains and are the central component of the progressive rehabilitation programme.
dailyManagement: Complete the prescribed rehabilitation exercise session (usually 20-30 minutes) at least five days per week; consistency matters more than individual session intensity.
Apply patellar tape before activities that provoke symptoms; reapply if the tape loses adhesion during a run.
Track pain during and 24 hours after each run on a 0-10 scale; increase load when pain stays below 3/10 and remains the same or lower the next day.
Log training mileage and note conditions (surface, elevation, fatigue level) to identify factors that reliably aggravate or protect symptoms.
Progress rehabilitation exercises every 2 weeks once the current level is pain-free and symptoms have not worsened for 5 consecutive days.
Seek reassessment if pain exceeds 5/10 on a run, increases progressively over consecutive sessions, or does not improve within 4 weeks of structured rehabilitation.
prognosis: The prognosis for runner's knee is generally favourable but requires patience. Approximately 80-90% of patients achieve clinically significant long-term improvement with consistent conservative rehabilitation. However, recovery is not linear: a Cochrane review found that at 6-12 months follow-up, about 30-40% of patients still report some residual pain, and the condition can become chronic if mechanical risk factors are not corrected. Athletes who address hip and quadriceps weakness, correct biomechanical faults, and manage training load effectively have the best outcomes. The condition rarely leads to surgical intervention — fewer than 5% of PFPS patients require surgery — and when it does, results are variable. Young active individuals generally recover faster and more completely than sedentary or older patients. Recurrence is common (approximately 25-40% over two years) if the precipitating training errors or muscle deficits are not permanently corrected.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients with moderate PFPS who begin structured rehabilitation experience a noticeable reduction in pain within 3-6 weeks. Return to pain-free running at previous volume typically takes 6-12 weeks for mild-to-moderate presentations and 3-6 months for severe or chronic cases. The rehabilitation progression follows: acute symptom management (weeks 1-3) → graded strength training (weeks 3-8) → sport-specific loading and return to running protocol (weeks 6-12) → full return to previous training level (weeks 10-16 for most runners). Athletes should expect some pain fluctuation during the process; a structured progressive return-to-running programme reduces the risk of setbacks.
complications: Chronicity — without addressing the mechanical root cause, PFPS can persist for 12+ months; approximately 30-40% of patients still report pain at 1 year follow-up in prospective studies.

Quadriceps inhibition and muscle wasting — chronic pain suppresses quadriceps activation, leading to progressive atrophy that further worsens patellofemoral biomechanics and creates a self-perpetuating cycle.

Secondary patellofemoral osteoarthritis — repeated abnormal loading over years may accelerate cartilage loss on the patellar articular surface; however, evidence directly linking PFPS to long-term osteoarthritis is moderate-quality.

Patellar dislocation — in runners with underlying structural laxity or trochlear dysplasia, inadequately managed PFPS can progress to acute patellar dislocation requiring medial patellofemoral ligament repair.

Psychological impact and loss of training fitness — chronic pain associated with a central runner identity produces anxiety, depression, and deconditioning; mental health support is relevant in long-standing cases.

supportResources.name: British Journal of Sports Medicine — Patellofemoral Pain Consensus Statement 2019
supportResources.description: International evidence-based consensus document on the diagnosis, treatment, and return-to-sport criteria for patellofemoral pain, open access.

supportResources.name: NHS — Knee pain
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient-facing guidance on knee pain self-care, when to seek help, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — Runner's Knee
supportResources.description: Evidence-based AAOS patient guide on runner's knee symptoms, diagnosis, and rehabilitation.

sources.title: Collins NJ et al. 2018 Consensus Statement on Exercise Therapy and Physical Interventions for Patellofemoral Pain. British Journal of Sports Medicine, 2018
Lack S et al. Proximal muscle rehabilitation is effective for patellofemoral pain: a systematic review with meta-analysis. British Journal of Sports Medicine, 2015
Halabchi F et al. Patellofemoral Pain in Athletes. Journal of Sport Rehabilitation, 2017
Barton CJ et al. Running retraining to treat lower limb injuries: a mixed-methods study. British Journal of Sports Medicine, 2016
Willy RW et al. Patellofemoral Pain — Clinical Practice Guidelines. Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy, 2019
NICE — Knee pain in the young (CKS)
American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — OrthoInfo: Runner's Knee

confusedWithConditions.name: Iliotibial band syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IT band syndrome causes pain at the lateral knee (outside of the knee) at a precise point 30 degrees into flexion and is reproduced by the Ober test and lateral compression test; PFPS pain is anterior and peripatellar, not lateral.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meniscal tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A meniscal tear typically presents with joint-line tenderness on the medial or lateral knee, a positive McMurray or Thessaly test, and often an effusion; PFPS lacks joint-line tenderness and typically has no effusion or mechanical locking.

confusedWithConditions.name: Knee osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Knee OA causes progressive diffuse joint-line and tibiofemoral pain with morning stiffness lasting over 30 minutes, joint-space narrowing on radiograph, and predominantly affects people over 50; PFPS is anterior and typically affects younger active individuals with normal joint space.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chondromalacia patellae
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chondromalacia patellae refers specifically to cartilage softening and fibrillation confirmed on MRI or arthroscopy; PFPS is a clinical pain syndrome that may or may not involve cartilage damage and is diagnosed clinically rather than by imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Shin splints (medial tibial stress syndrome)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both PFPS and shin splints are overuse injuries driven by rapid training load increases and biomechanical faults; they frequently co-exist in runners increasing mileage too quickly.

relatedConditions.name: Knee osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-standing patellofemoral malalignment and cartilage loading may accelerate the development of patellofemoral osteoarthritis; OA must be excluded in patients over 45 years presenting with anterior knee pain.

relatedConditions.name: Meniscal tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Meniscal pathology is a key differential for persistent knee pain not responding to PFPS rehabilitation and must be excluded by MRI in runners failing 12 weeks of conservative treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions are common running overuse injuries driven by biomechanical factors; excessive foot pronation that contributes to PFPS also loads the plantar fascia, and the two conditions are commonly found together in the same runner.

relatedConditions.name: Iliotibial band syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: IT band syndrome and PFPS are the two most prevalent running overuse knee injuries, share overlapping biomechanical risk factors (hip weakness, training errors), and frequently co-occur in the same athlete.

faqs.question: What is runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Runner's knee (patellofemoral pain syndrome) is pain around or behind the kneecap caused by increased stress on the patellofemoral joint during running, squatting, or stair use. It is the most common knee injury in runners, accounting for 25-40% of all knee complaints in sports medicine.

faqs.question: Can I keep running with runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases. Reduce running volume to a level that keeps pain below 3/10 on a scale of 10 rather than stopping completely. Maintaining some running while addressing the cause produces better outcomes than total rest. Cross-train with swimming or cycling to preserve fitness.

faqs.question: How long does runner's knee take to heal?
faqs.answer: Mild to moderate cases improve significantly in 6-12 weeks with consistent rehabilitation. Severe or chronic cases can take 3-6 months. Recovery requires active rehabilitation — not just rest. Roughly 80-90% of patients achieve substantial long-term improvement with proper conservative management.

faqs.question: What exercises help runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Hip strengthening (clamshells, side-lying hip abduction, single-leg bridges) and quadriceps strengthening (terminal knee extension, step-downs, wall sits) are the highest-evidence exercises for runner's knee. Perform these 5 days per week for at least 8-12 weeks as the foundation of recovery.

faqs.question: Does runner's knee require surgery?
faqs.answer: Fewer than 5% of runner's knee cases require surgery. Conservative rehabilitation resolves symptoms in the vast majority of runners. Surgery is only considered after at least 6 months of properly supervised rehabilitation has failed and structural pathology such as severe chondromalacia or trochlear dysplasia has been confirmed on MRI.

faqs.question: Is running bad for knee cartilage?
faqs.answer: Moderate running is not harmful to knee cartilage and may be protective. Recreational runners have a lower prevalence of knee osteoarthritis (3.5%) than sedentary people (10.2%), according to systematic reviews. The problem is rapid load increases exceeding the joint's adaptation capacity, not running itself.

faqs.question: Why does sitting make runner's knee worse?
faqs.answer: Prolonged knee flexion while sitting maintains the patella compressed against the femoral trochlea at a constant angle, elevating joint pressure over time. This is called the 'theatre sign' or 'cinema sign' and is one of the most reliable diagnostic features of patellofemoral pain. Taking regular standing breaks relieves it.

faqs.question: What is the cause of runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Runner's knee results from increased patellofemoral joint stress, most often from a combination of weak hip and quadriceps muscles, rapid training load increases, and biomechanical faults such as excessive knee valgus or foot pronation during running. No single cause applies to every runner.

faqs.question: Should I see a physiotherapist for runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Yes. A physiotherapist experienced in running injuries can identify the specific biomechanical contributors — hip weakness, gait faults, foot mechanics — and prescribe a targeted rehabilitation programme. This is significantly more effective than generic rest or over-the-counter treatments alone.

faqs.question: Does patellar taping help runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Yes. McConnell patellar taping reduces pain by approximately 30-50% immediately after application in clinical trials by correcting patellar tracking. It is used as a short-term pain-relief adjunct in the first 4-8 weeks of rehabilitation to allow higher-load exercise, not as a standalone treatment.

faqs.question: Can running shoes cause runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Worn-out shoes (over 500-700 km) lose midsole cushioning and shock attenuation, increasing peak loading rates. Shoes that allow excessive rearfoot pronation can contribute to patellofemoral malalignment. Replacing footwear regularly and considering motion control shoes or orthotics is a useful preventive step for susceptible runners.

faqs.question: What is the difference between runner's knee and jumper's knee?
faqs.answer: Runner's knee (PFPS) produces diffuse pain around the kneecap that worsens with sitting, squatting, and running. Jumper's knee (patellar tendinopathy) causes focal pain at the inferior pole of the kneecap that worsens specifically with jumping and is tender to precise palpation at the tendon insertion — a different location and mechanism.

faqs.question: Does ice help runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Ice (an ice pack or frozen peas wrapped in a cloth) applied for 15-20 minutes after exercise can reduce acute periarticular pain and swelling during the inflammatory phase. It provides symptomatic relief but does not treat the underlying cause. Avoid applying ice directly to skin. Its benefit beyond 2-3 weeks of use is limited.

faqs.question: Can weak hips cause runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Yes. Weak hip abductors and external rotators (particularly gluteus medius) allow the femur to internally rotate and adduct excessively during running stance phase, shifting the patella laterally off-centre in the trochlear groove. Hip strengthening is the single most evidence-supported intervention for correcting this root cause.

faqs.question: Is runner's knee the same as chondromalacia patellae?
faqs.answer: Not exactly. Chondromalacia patellae refers to structural softening and fibrillation of the patellar cartilage confirmed on MRI or arthroscopy. Runner's knee (PFPS) is a clinical pain diagnosis that does not require cartilage damage — many patients have normal cartilage but still have significant pain from elevated joint stress.

faqs.question: How can I prevent runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Key prevention strategies are: not increasing weekly mileage by more than 10% per week; strengthening hip abductors and quadriceps as part of every training cycle; replacing running shoes every 500-700 km; maintaining running cadence at 170-180 steps per minute; and addressing foot pronation with orthotics if relevant.

faqs.question: What cross-training is safe with runner's knee?
faqs.answer: Pool running (aqua jogging), swimming, road cycling with a raised saddle, and elliptical training are all safe and effective cross-training options for runners with active PFPS. They maintain cardiovascular fitness without loading the patellofemoral joint. Avoid cycling with a low saddle and deep-water squatting.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for runner's knee?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if knee pain is above 5/10 during normal activity, there is significant visible swelling of the knee joint, symptoms do not improve after 4 weeks of self-directed rest and basic exercises, or if a traumatic event caused sudden severe pain suggesting dislocation, ligament rupture, or stress fracture.

metaTitle: Runner's Knee: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Runner's knee causes aching kneecap pain during running, stairs and sitting. Learn causes, rehab exercises and how to return to sport. Doctor-reviewed.
